text: The surgeon begins the procedure by accessing the retro muscular space, preparing for the initial steps of the operation.
The surgeon inserts a KII FiOS first entry trocar into the surgical field, preparing for access to the retro muscular space.
The KII FiOS first entry trocar is shown with a small fenestration at its tip, allowing for insufflation while the inner cannula remains in position.
The surgeon dissects parallel to the rectus muscle to gain access to the retro rectus space, with visible manipulation of the tissue layers adjacent to the rectus muscle.
The surgeon initiates insufflation upon visualizing the target area, followed by blunt dissection to open up space for trocar placement.
The surgeon performs side docking after completing blunt dissection in the retromuscular space, positioning the trocar for further surgical access.
The video shows the placement of the lowermost trocar centrally within the muscle, which is visually identified as being in a suboptimal position.
The surgeon, after docking, removes areolar tissue to expand the retromuscular space on the initial entry side.
The surgeon works to fully expose the posterior sheath, revealing the underlying anatomical structures within the retromuscular space.
The video shows the initial appearance of a hernia, with the surgeon focusing on maximizing the dissection in the retromuscular space before proceeding to the next step of crossover.
The video shows a clear view of the retromuscular space, facilitated by the wide rectus muscles, providing easy visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the entry point into the retromuscular space on the contralateral side, ensuring safe entry without damaging the visible linea alba during the diastasis repair.
The surgeon extends the incision, carefully dropping the preperitoneal fat to avoid involving the peritoneum while opening the sheath.
The surgeon opens the posterior sheath with minimal retromuscular dissection, focusing on a limited initial exposure.
The surgeon approaches the hernia sac, encountering tougher tissue during dissection. A hole is made in the peritoneum, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully opens the sheath, minimizing the use of energy to avoid thermal injury, while focusing on precise dissection.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the hernia sac, checking for any viscera adhered to it. The dissection proceeds smoothly, with some energy used to aid in the process.

The surgeon begins to drop the hernia sac, alternating between dropping the peritoneum and identifying the medial aspect. The posterior sheath is taken on the contralateral side, approaching the linea alba.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the contralateral semilunar line, working within the retro rectus space, ensuring adequate exposure despite potential challenges with narrow rectus muscles.
The surgeon proceeds to work around the central area of the hernia, identified as an M2, M3 hernia, carefully navigating the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies a transverse branch off the left inferior epigastric artery, a consistent anatomical marker when approaching M3 hernias.
The surgeon encounters challenging dissection due to visible scar tissue and carefully aims to enter the preperitoneal space to avoid injuring the semilunar line.
The surgeon performs contralateral retro-rectus dissection, carefully removing areolar tissue while progressing towards the semilunar line.
The surgeon extends the retrorectus dissection inferiorly towards the inferior epigastric vessels, carefully removing areolar tissue and ensuring the fat is separated from the posterior sheath.
The surgeon ensures that the fat remains attached to the epigastric vessels while preparing to visualize the transverse branch that penetrates towards the hernia.
The surgeon identifies the transverse branch and carefully preserves it while working to establish a clearer dissection plane around the hernia.
The surgeon encounters minor bleeding during the dissection process, requiring careful management to maintain visibility and control within the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on unifying the retromuscular dissection on both the left and right sides, ensuring the tissue planes are connected across the surgical field.
The surgeon performs ipsilateral retromuscular dissection, achieving good exposure that facilitates extensive dissection from the side dock.
The surgeon prepares to close a small hole in the posterior layer using a running V-lock suture, specifically a two-hole V-lock technique.
The surgeon begins stitching superiorly in the middle of the diastasis, expanding the sutures outward in a Christmas tree pattern.
The surgeon grabs the sheaths, taking relatively small bites when stitching the diastasis, and larger bites when reaching the fascia associated with the hernia.

The surgeon performs sequential tightening of the sutures, observing the abdominal wall for tension. In this case, the sutures are fully tightened from the beginning due to the absence of significant tension.
The surgeon takes larger bites of the fascia as they approach and work within the hernia, ensuring full-thickness closure. The fascia is closed, and the muscle edges are closely approximated.
The surgeon cuts the suture and prepares the surgical field for mesh placement, following the closure of the fascia and muscle layers.

The surgeon rolls up the mesh tightly, secures it at a few points, and then unrolls it within the surgical field. The sticky side of the BARDsoft mesh is placed downwards, aiding in the unrolling process similar to laying out a carpet.
The surgeon applies Tissio to cover the surgical area, completing the operation.
